coredata.dc:title: N,N-Dimethylformamide solvent assisted hydrothermal pretreatment of Chlorella for coproduction of sugar, nitrogenous compounds and carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Linghao
Zhang, Fan
Tu, Ren
Yu, Haipeng
Wang, Hong
Sun, Yan
Jiang, Enchen
Xu, Xiwei
coredata.dc:description: Microalgae are considered as a promising alternative to fossil fuels due to their ease of cultivation, short growth cycle and no occupation of cultivated land. In this study, N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) solvent was employed to assist hydrothermal pretreatment of Chlorella for coproduction of sugar, nitrogenous compounds and carbon dots (CDs). The effect of pretreatment conditions on the composition and pyrolysis bio-oil distribution of hydrothermal solid residues as well as CDs characteristic were investigated by varying the temperature (180–220 ℃) and reaction time (1–9 h). The results showed that pretreated residues had higher cellulose. And the yield of sugar and N-contained compounds reached 41.59% and 63.57% in the pyrolysis bio-oil of pretreated algae residues, respectively. Moreover, CDs obtained from hydrothermal solution fluoresced red under 365 nm excitation. The paper provides a new method for the complete utilization of microalgae.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Algae
DMF-assisted Hydrothermal pretreatment
Sugars
Nitrogenous compounds
Carbon dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China , China (Grant No. 52076083 ); Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province , China (Grant No. 2021A1515010886 ); Youth science and technology innovation talent of Guangdong TeZhi plan China (Grant No. 2019TQ05N068 ); and Rural Revitalization Strategy Special Fund (“Special Project + Task List”) project in Yunfu City, Guangdong Province, China (Grant No. 2020020203 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TG and DTG curves of pretreated samples ans raw materials. (a) TG of raw material and pretreated in water or DMF at 200℃ for 5 h. (b) DTG of raw material and pretreated in water or DMF at 200℃ for 5 h. (c) TG of Chlorella pretreated in DMF at different temperature for 7 h. (d) DTG of Chlorella pretreated in DMF at different temperature for 7 h. (e) TG of Chlorella pretreated in DMF at 200 °C for different time. (f) DTG of Chlorella pretreated in DMF at 200 °C for different time.

The relative content of bio-oil components produced from pyrolysis of raw and pretreated Chlorella: (a) raw and pretreated in different solvent, (b) pretreated in DMF at 200℃ with different time, (c) pretreated in DMF at different temperature for 7 h.
The relative content of carbohydrate components produced from pyrolysis of DMF pretreated Chlorella in different temperature and time.
The relative content of N components produced from pyrolysis of DMF pretreated Chlorella at different hydrothermal temperature and time.
Reaction paths of sugar and N-contained compounds.
A list of element components and properties analysis of Chlorella (wt.%, dry basis).
A list of thermogravimetric analyses of raw and pretreated biomass.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Ti means the initial pyrolysis temperature, Wmax means the maximum weight loss rate, temperature, Ychar means the mass loss. Tmax means the corresponding temperature for maximum weight loss peak, Tf means the final pyrolysis temperature.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A list of components and properties analysis of pretreated Chlorella. (wt.%, dry basis).

head.ce:title: N,N-Dimethylformamide solvent assisted hydrothermal pretreatment of Chlorella for coproduction of sugar, nitrogenous compounds and carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, 483 Wush-an Road, Guangzhou 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, 483 Wush-an Road, Guangzhou 510642, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microalgae are considered as a promising alternative to fossil fuels due to their ease of cultivation, short growth cycle and no occupation of cultivated land. In this study, N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) solvent was employed to assist hydrothermal pretreatment of Chlorella for coproduction of sugar, nitrogenous compounds and carbon dots (CDs). The effect of pretreatment conditions on the composition and pyrolysis bio-oil distribution of hydrothermal solid residues as well as CDs characteristic were investigated by varying the temperature (180–220 ℃) and reaction time (1–9 h). The results showed that pretreated residues had higher cellulose. And the yield of sugar and N-contained compounds reached 41.59% and 63.57% in the pyrolysis bio-oil of pretreated algae residues, respectively. Moreover, CDs obtained from hydrothermal solution fluoresced red under 365 nm excitation. The paper provides a new method for the complete utilization of microalgae.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: With carbon peaking and carbon neutrality on the agenda of some countries, replacing traditional fossil sources of carbon with green, renewable sources to refine chemicals is gradually attracting the attention of researchers. Algae, are the third generation of green and renewable energy crops as they are fast growing, require minimal inputs, and do not compete for land (Chia et al., 2018).

Currently, algae were considered as an important resource for biofuels and a limited alternative to fossil fuels due to their lipid-rich and simple structure (Ansari et al., 2015). However, the applications of proteins and carbohydrates, which were also the main components of algae, were rarely studied. In contrast, proteins contain N elements that are considered as harmful to the quality of bio-oil due to the NOx emission during combustion. However, recently, some researchers suggested that some nitrogen-containing heterocycles in bio-oil had potential applications in the synthesis of pharmaceuticals (Chen et al., 2017; Tian et al., 2014; Zhu et al., 2016). Moreover, it was found that cyanides and amines can be easily converted into straight-chain hydrocarbons in bio-oil hydroprocessing (Li et al., 2021).

Furthermore, the research of bottom-up synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from biomass as a source has also received much attention (Liu et al., 2019). Algae can be used not only as a carbon source but also as a nitrogen source due to the proteins for CDs. Especially, CDs are regarded as promising candidates for various applications, including sensors, optoelectronic devices, photocatalysis, anti-counterfeiting, and bioimaging use (Lv et al., 2021). Moreover, compared to most of blue to green fluorescence emission CDs, red fluorescence CDs is important in the bioimaging field, because it dose not interfered with autofluorescence of biological tissue (Kuo et al., 2015). And red phosphor is one of the indispensable components for the fabrication of full-color and white light-emitting diodes (Song et al., 2019).

Carbohydrates are mainly derived from cellulose and pectin in the cell wall of algae, which can be converted into sugars platform chemicals such as xylan, galactose and glucose. Moreover, these sugars can be chemically or biologically converted into C2-C6 structural units (e.g., ethanol, acetone, butanol, furfural, and sorbitol) for further derivatives (Zhou et al., 2021). Especially, Levoglucosan (LG) is a 1,6-anhydro derivative of beta-d-glucopyranose, which is widely used in the chemical industry for the manufacture of plastics, surfactants, pharmaceuticals, propellants and resins, and as a saving substitute for materials such as sorbitol (Liu et al., 2019).

Currently, the effects of various parameters such as time, temperature, solid–liquid ratio of hydrothermal techniques on crude bio-oil for targeting lipids have been widely studied (Biswas et al., 2017; Castro et al., 2021). However, the traditional hydrothermal extraction used water as solvent, resulting in the hydrolysis of most organic matter in the algae. In this study, DMF was chosen to assist hydrothermal pretreatment, enabling the separation of hydrocarbons and protein. Therefore, the hydrocarbons remained in the solid production, while protein was involved in the formation of carbon spots in the liquid. Especially, it was found that the cellulose enriched solids can be converted to bio-oil rich with LG via fast pyrolysis (Liu et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2020; Jiang et al., 2017). The fast pyrolysis process uses a short time without enzymes or catalysts to overcome the recalcitrance of biomass and releases valuable fermentable sugars, which has a lower capital investment compared to biochemical routes. Moreover, DMF with amide structure can undergo an ester exchange-like reaction with proteins in Chlorella to obtain N-containing chemicals.

In view of the above considerations, Chlorella with a high protein and carbohydrate and DMF solvent were selected for DMF-assisted hydrothermal pretreatment to obtain high value pyrolysis bio-oil rich with sugar and nitrogenous compounds and carbon dots (CDs). Furthermore, the effect of pretreatment conditions on the composition and distribution of pyrolysis bio-oil of hydrothermal solid residues as well as CDs characteristic were also investigated. The paper developed a promising pretreatment means to achieve the full component utilization of Chlorella.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Materials
ce:section.ce:para: Chlorella was provided by Shengqing Biotechnology Co.Ltd.(Shanxi, China). N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) was analytical reagent grade and was purchased from Richjoint (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methods
The DMF solvent assisted hydrothermal pretreatment
ce:section.ce:para: In this experiment, hydrothermal reaction was carried out in a 500 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Especially, 5 g of Chlorella and 50 g solvent (water or DMF) were charged into the Teflon-lined. Before heating to the reaction temperature, the mixture was stirred at 600 rpm on a magnetic stirrer for 30 min for fully contacting of Chlorella and solvent. And then, the sealed autoclave was put into oven, and heated to reaction temperature at the heating rate of about 15℃/min. Once the reaction was completed, the reactor was put into cool water till it cool to the room temperature. The solid–liquid mixture was separated by a vacuum suction filter (SHZ-D). The liquid product which contained CDs was collected in the sample vials, and the solid product was washed by alcohol and then dried at 105°C for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The pyrolysis of hydrothermal solid products
ce:section.ce:para: The pyrolysis happened in the fixed bed reactor with a quartz tube, which was similar to the previous research (Zhang et al., 2021). The diameter and lent of the quartz tube were 40 mm and 500 mm, respectively. 500 mg hydrothermal carbon was placed in the middle of quartz tube and the N2 was taken to discharge air. When the reactor reached about 500℃, put the quartz tube into the reactor, and 550 mL/min N2 was taken as the carrier gas. The pyrolysis volatile mixed with the N2 went through the reactor and cooling equipment quickly. A round bottom flask that filled with acetone was linked after cooling equipment to collect bio-oil.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Products characterization analysis
The composition and structure analysis of hydrothermal solid products
ce:section.ce:para: Thermogravimetric analysis of Chlorella and hydrothermal solid products was performed on a Netzsch STA449C thermogravimeter. Using 8–12 mg Chlorella or hydrothermal pretreated samples, the temperature was increased from 20℃ to 900℃ at 15℃/min under the condition of 60 mL/min N2 as protective gas. FT-IR was tested by the Brook Vertex70 Fourier Infrared Spectrometer with a scanning range of 400–4000 cm−1 and a scanning rate of 2 cm−1. The elemental composition of the samples was determined by Elementar's Vario El Cube elemental analyzer. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) of the sample was recorded by Rigaku Ultima IV diffractometer in the 2θ range of 5–90 ° at a scanning rate of 10°/min. Protein content was determined by Dumas method. Lipid content was measured by Sohlet method. Proximate analysis was conducted based on GB/T 28731–2012.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The composition analysis of bio-oil of solid products pyrolysis
ce:section.ce:para: The components of bio-oil obtained after pyrolysis were determined by Agilent 7890B-5977A GC–MS system, the method was similar to the previous research (Zhang et al., 2021). The carrier gas was He, and the flow rate was 1 mL/min. The initial temperature was 40℃, keeping for 5 min, and then it increased to 150℃ at the heating rate of 10℃/min. Then, it increased to 200℃ at the rate of 5℃/min and kept for 5 min. Finally, it was heated to 280℃ at the rate of 10℃/min and maintained for 8 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of carbon dots derived from the hydrothermal solution
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs were measured with an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (Shanghai Youke UV762). Fluorescence spectra were measured using a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The morphology of the CDts was observed under an FEI Talos F200S transmission electron microscope. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded on a Thermo-Scientific Ecalab 250Xi spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussions
The composition analysis of Chlorella
ce:section.ce:para: The elemental and component analysis of dried Chlorella are listed in Table 1 , respectively. It is clear that nitrogen and sulfur are rich in Chlorella compared to ligno-cellulosic biomass, whereas carbon content is within the same range. The high nitrogen (9.94%) and sulfur (0.84%) are contributed to the 61.44% protein and Chlorophyll. The abundant hydroxyl, ether, ester and carbonyl groups in carbohydrates and lipids are the source of the O element. The high O/C ratio is not conducive to direct use of Chlorella as a fuel, while the abundance of N makes it promising for obtaining nitrogen-doped CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and analysis of hydrothermal solid products
Thermal decomposition behavior of hydrothermal solid products
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the composition of hydrothermal residues and determine the fast pyrolysis temperature, the thermogravimetric was carried out. As shown in Fig. 1 , the TG and DTG curves of the raw Chlorella showed three thermal decomposition steps. In the early stage of decomposition, Chlorella showed a small peak below 200 °C due to the release of water and low volatile materials (Gan et al., 2021). There were two overlapping peaks between 290 °C and 319 °C, corresponding to the decomposition of carbohydrates and proteins in Chlorella, respectively (Wang et al., 2017a). In the third stage, a shoulder peak appeared at 400 °C due to the lower lipid content in Chlorella. After hydrothermal in water at 200℃ for 5 h, the peak disappeared around 290 °C, indicating that carbohydrates were hydrolyzed (Chen et al., 2014). The formation of carbon polymers during hydrothermal processes and undecomposed lipid led to a distinct shoulder peak at 430 °C (Gan et al., 2021). The TG and DTG curves of hydrothermal solid products at 200℃ for 5 h in DMF solvent were significantly different from the original and hydrothermal solid sample in water solvent. The higher cellulose content in the sample after DMF-assisted hydrothermal pretreatment led to a weight loss rate increase from 6.78%/min to 10.04%/min at 295℃. The dissolution or decomposition of lipids resulted in the disappearance of the corresponding peak at 400 °C, while the appearance of a small peak at 450 °C was due to the formation of carbon polymers.

The pretreated time and temperature had the similar influence on the thermal decomposition behavior of hydrothermal solid products (Fig. 1(d) and (f)). As shown in Fig. 1(d), Chlorella pretreated at 200 °C have the highest Wmax (10.61%/min), indicating higher cellulose content than the samples pretreated at 180 °C and 220 °C. The total weight losses in the range of 400–500 °C increase with pretreated temperature from 180 to 200 °C. This was attributed to the formation of products from the hydrolysis of small molecules by hydrolysis and solvent polymerization during the DMF-assisted hydrothermal pretreatment. Fig. 1(f) shows the effect of reaction time on the thermal decomposition behavior of hydrothermal solid products, and the weight loss rate corresponding to cellulose decomposition rises from 7.91%/min to 10.61%/min when the pretreated time increased from 1 h to 7 h and then it falls to 4.38%/min. It can be explained by the fact that at the first 7 h, the cellulose was enriched due to the decomposition and dissolution of lipids and proteins in Chlorella. However, when the pretreated time is over 7 h, the carbohydrates was thermally decomposed to low-molecular carbohydrate,which further promoted the formation of carbon polymers (Jiang et al., 2020). More thermostable structure was formed after DMF participating in the polymerization process. It is the reason that the corresponding peaks shifted to the high temperature zone around 460℃ when the time increased to 9 h. Moreover, the total mass loss decreased from 63.58% to 37.91% (Table 2 ) when pretreated time increased from 1 h to 9 h due to the increased ash content in residual solids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Component and proximate analysis of hydrothermal solid products
ce:section.ce:para: The component and proximate analysis of pretreated Chlorella are shown in Table 3 . The cellulose content increased from 13.39% to 33.37% with an increase in DMF-assisted hydrothermal time from 1 h to 5 h. It is possible that DMF formed a strong solvation shell around cellulose which prevented the hydrolysis of cellulose via isolating cellulose and water from polymerization reactions. However, when the pretreated time was extended from 5 h to 9 h, slowly thermal decomposition caused the decrease of cellulose content from 33.37% to 29.16%. The change trend of cellulose content with temperature is similar to that of time. Especially, when the pretreated temperature increased from 180℃ to 200℃ then to 220℃, the cellulose content first increased from 27.05% to 33.06% and then decreased to 16.61%. Hemicellulose gradually decreased from 2.34% to 1.33% with pretreated time prolonging from 1 h to 9 h, because hydrogen bond between hemicellulose and cellulose was destroyed and part of hemicellulose was decomposed at 200 °C. DMF was a polar aprotic solvent with high solvency capacity, which can dissolve lignin causing a decrease in lignin content (Yin et al., 2021). Protein content decreased from 70.98% to 51.57% when time prolonged from 1 h to 7 h, and then it increased to 60.12% at 9 h. After hydrothermal pretreatment, cellulose and hemicellulose in HTL-200℃-5h sample were hydrolyzed. However, the hydrolysis products occurred polymerization and formed pseudolignin (Kapoor et al., 2017), leading to a significant increase in lignin content from 20.54% to 78.88%.

The main source of volatiles is cellulose, while hemicellulose and lignin contribute greatly to the formation of fixed carbon (Cai et al., 2020). Therefore, the changes of lignin and hemicellulose resulted in fixed carbon content decreased from 15.51% to 7.37% when temperature increased from 180℃ to 220℃. Although DMF-200 °C-7 h sample contained the most cellulose, its volatile was not the highest, probably due to the removal of more proteins. Additionally, the increase of ash indicated that the weak removal of ash and organic component was continuously decomposed during the pretreatment. In contrast, the ash contained only 7.25% in HTL-200℃-5h sample. And the high fixed carbon production was caused by pseudolignin formed during the hydrothermal pretreatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface groups analysis of hydrothermal solid products via FT-IR
ce:section.ce:para: The pretreated Chlorella samples showed more abundant surface groups than the original sample. The broad peaks of the original Chlorella sample between 3033 and 3867 cm−1 belong to the O–H and N–H from the carbohydrate and protein fractions therein, respectively. The absorption peaks at 2921 cm−1 and 2851 cm−1 are due to the presence of a “lipid band spectrum” associated with symmetric C–H stretching of methylene (Biswas et al., 2017b). The peak at 1659 cm−1 corresponds to the C = O stretching of amide I, and the peak at 1543 cm−1 corresponds to the N–H bending vibration of amide II. And the vibrations of these peaks are generated by the peptide bonds in the protein (Sultana et al., 2020). Peaks at ∼ 1455 cm−1 and ∼ 1398 cm−1 are designated as CH3– and CH2– in the protein, respectively (Giordano et al., 2001). The peak at ∼ 1230 cm−1 is from the P = O vibration of the phosphodiester backbone or phosphate in nucleic acids. Carbohydrates are characterized by weak and moderate characteristic bands at 1150–839 cm−1 (Giordano et al., 2001). The change in the intensity of these peaks after pretreatment is related to the formation and breakage of their representative bond. After hydrothermal pretreatment at 200 °C for 5 h, the bands corresponding to carbohydrates were significantly weakened. However, the peaks intensities of carbohydrates were enhanced after pretreatment under the same conditions with DMF-assisted hydrothermal, indicating that the cellulose structure was preserved in the DMF solvent environment. During hydrothermal pretreatment, lipids, carbohydrates and low thermal resistance proteins in Chlorella are easily hydrolyzed to low molecular weight substances, containing carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino groups. Then, a complex polymerization reacted among those molecular to form new C = O structures, resulting in a different peak shape at around 1650 cm−1. Moreover, the DMF-200℃-5h sample showed two sharp peaks at 1698 cm−1 and 1673 cm−1 may be caused by the repolymerization of the depolymerization products with the (CH3)2N– structure in the solvent.

The effect of pretreated temperature and time on surface groups of the DMF-assisted hydrolysis residues was studied. It can be observed that the N–H vibrations of amide II weakened after 5 h pretreatment, which may be related to the loss of protein in the solid residue. As the DMF-assisted hydrothermal pretreatment retention time increased from 1 h to 5 h at 200 °C, the peaks intensities of the carbohydrates (1150–839 cm−1) increased. When pretreated time prolonged from 5 to 9 h, the peaks intensities of carbohydrates decreased. The increased process was attributed to the cellulose retention during DMF-assisted hydrothermal pretreatment before 5 h, while thermal decomposition caused the decrease after 5 h. The absorption peaks intensity at 1455 and 1398 cm−1 corresponding to CH3– and CH2– groups in protein increased with the pretreated temperature increasing from 180 to 220 ℃. However, the content of protein decreased when the temperature increased. Therefore, it was indicated the change in the intensity of the two absorption peaks was attributed to the (CH3)2N– groups from DMF, which was grafted on the pretreated samples surfaces during pretreatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The crystallinity analysis of hydrothermal solid products via XRD
ce:section.ce:para: Different pretreatment conditions disrupt the biomass structure differently and alter their crystallinity. Cellulose is the main component of the cell wall in Chlorella, and starch can be synthesized by chloroplasts. The diffraction peaks of Chlorella were observed mainly among 18 and 21°, which was similar to the results of (Gan et al., 2020). The peaks observed at 9.54° and 19.64°, belonging to the α-helix and β-sheet molecular structures of protein, respectively (Gu et al., 2020). The crystallinity of the samples decreased from 13.20% to 12.08% corresponding to the destruction of the protein secondary structure. The curve of α-helix and β-sheet peaks diminished with increasing pretreated time and temperature, indicating that depolymerization of protein structure by solvent. The crystallinity of samples increased from 13.53% to 22.23% when time increased from 5 h to 9 h, which was due to the peak of revealed cellulose. Because crystalline cellulose was difficult to be destroyed during the DMF-assisted pretreatment and amorphous carbohydrates was removed. The peaks between 25° and 30° were due to the presence of mineral components in Chlorella, mainly consisted of CaCO3, K2SO4 and KAlSi3O8, etc (Tarchoun et al., 2019). The mineral components were insoluble in DMF solvent, and the hydrothermal temperature and time had slightly effect on the diffraction peaks. However, during hydrothermal pretreatment of Chlorella in the water, the cell structure is destroyed, which leads to the release of mineral components and the significant decrease of the corresponding diffraction peaks.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of DMF-assisted pretreatment on pyrolytic bio-oil composition of solid products
The distribution of pyrolytic bio-oil products
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrothermal pretreated Chlorella at different solvent, temperature and time was pyrolyzed at 500℃, Fig. 2 showed the pyrolytic bio-oil distribution of untreated Chlorella and pretreated Chlorella. Chlorella pyrolytic bio-oil mainly contained nitrogenous components, hydrocarbons, alcohols and aromatic substances, as well as small amounts of carbohydrates and ketones. The N in the proteins of Chlorella is the main source of N-contained components in the bio-oil. Because Chlorella contained 61.44% protein, and the N-containing substance was the most abundant in bio-oil. Hydrocarbon was derived from two aspects 1)Proteins were broken into small molecules of amino acids, which then underwent decarboxylation and deamination reactions and formed hydrocarbon. 2)Similarly, decarboxylation of lipids was also a source of hydrocarbon (Wang et al., 2017a). Carbohydrates were mainly from the decomposition of cellulose and hemicellulose in the cell wall and starch in chloroplasts. Moreover, aromatic structures were related to lignin in the cell wall, and alcohols were mainly from the decomposition of cell membranes and intracellular lipids (Wang et al., 2017b). Compared with the untreated Chlorella, the HTL-200℃-5h sample pyrolysis did not produce carbohydrates, as a result of cellulose and hemicellulose were hydrolyzed. The Melad reaction that occurred during hydrothermal process formed nitrogenous coke, which was converted to nitrogenous heterocycles as well as other nitrogenous organic compounds during pyrolysis (Chen et al., 2017). The formation of pseudo-lignin was the main reason for the increase of aromatic ring-like structures in bio-oil.

Moreover, the content of nitrogen fraction first decreased from 50.63% to 29.07% with DMF-assisted hydrothermal time increasing from 1 h to 5 h, and then it increased to 46.88% at 9 h. Though the hemicellulose content decreased when the pretreated temperature increased, the carbohydrates content increased. Especially, the cellulose increased from 27.05% to 33.06% as the pretreated temperature increased from 180 ℃ to 200 ℃ (Table 3). Then the content of cellulose decreased to 16.61%, causing downward trend of the carbohydrates content in the bio-oil. The effect of the pretreated time had a similar change tendency on cellulose during DMF-assisted hydrothermal pretreatment. Therefore, carbohydrates and N-contained compounds had the opposite change trends with the pretreatment time and temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of DMF-assited pretreatment on sugar products distribution
ce:section.ce:para: The sugars in bio-oil from pyrolysis of the DMF pretreated samples were mainly including LG, pyranose and mannans (Fig. 3 ). The galactans from pectin pyrolysis were not distinguished with mannans due to the overlaps of their GC–MS peaks and the peak area was very small. LG was the main product of fast pyrolysis of cellulose (Lindstrom et al., 2019), and the reaction path is shown in eq (1) (Fig. 5). The pyranose was derived from α-anomers. Cellulose and cellobiose consist of glucose units linked by β-1,4-glycosidic bonds, they did not produce as many α-anomers as glucose during fast pyrolysis (Liu et al., 2014). Hence, pyranose is less than glucose in bio-oil. The mannans came from hemicellulose, linked with lignin. The most pronounced rose in LG was from 8.94% to 28.26% as the time extended from 3 h to 5 h, due to the significant removal of proteins and enrich of cellulose in the pretreated samples. With further extension of hydrothermal time, cellulose content decreased due to the thermal decomposition. Hence, when time increased from 7 h to 9 h, LG in pyrolysis bio-oil decreased from 29.17% to 20.74%. The effect of pretreated temperature on LG content is similar to that of pretreated time, both showing a high correlation with cellulose. However, the mannans did not show the same change trend with the hemicellulose content. With the pretreated time increasing from 1 h to 7 h, the mannans increased from 1.31% to 6.02%, while the hemicellulose decreased from 2.34% to 1.79%. It is possible that mannose is more stable than other components in hemicellulose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of DMF-assited pretreatment on N-contained compounds distribution
ce:section.ce:para:
Bio-oil produced by the pyrolysis of Chlorella contains a large number of nitrogenous components, including cyanides, amines, pyridine, pyrrole and other nitrogen heterocyclic and so on (Fig. 4
). Those nitrogen heterocyclic were important intermediates in the synthesis of some pharmaceuticals (Chen et al., 2017). And cyanides and amines can be easily converted into straight-chain hydrocarbons in bio-oil hydroprocessing (Li et al., 2021). Moreover, it was reported that nitrogen heterocyclic were formed via amino acids cyclization or peptide chain condensation reaction during pyrolysis, as shown in eqs (2) and (3) (Fig. 5) (Ma et al., 2019).

At the initial stage, the nitrogenous fraction was mainly derived from proteins. Thus, nitrogen heterocyclic compound accounts for the major nitrogen fraction in bio-oil, 27.70% and 29.96% at 1 h and 3 h, respectively. However, a large amount of organic matter, mainly containing proteins, was removed after 5 h DMF pretreatment(shown in table 3), which leaded to a 16.13% reduction of the nitrogen heterocyclic compound. It was reported that amides and amines derived from the proteolytic decomposition during the pyrolysis stage (Chen et al., 2017). And the amides was generated via the reaction of low thermal resistance proteins and solvents DMF during pretreatment, as shown in eq (4) (Fig. 5). The formation of nitrile is related to the cleavage of amino acids and the dehydration reaction of amide intermediates. Especially, dimethyl-Cyanamide was mainly a product of solvent with amino acids via the reaction pathways of eq (5) (Fig. 5). With the increase of time and temperature, dimethyl-Cyanamide also rose gradually due to that the proteins were continuously decomposed and gradually formed carbonpolymers during pretreatment. And the results was consistent with the change of proteins in the residuse.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristic of carbon dots derived from the hydrothermal solution
Fluorescence behaviors of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the luminescent properties of the CDs, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were performed. After pretreatment in DMF at 200℃ for 1 h, UV–vis absorption peaks were displayed at 220 nm and 408 nm due to the aromatic structure and conjugated polymer, respectively (Jeong et al., 2020). The aromatic structure is derived from the dissolved lignin, and it promoted the formation of graphite core. After 3 h, a broad absorption shoulder peak appeared around 270 nm, and it gradually strengthened with pretreated time. This shoulder peak can be assigned to various n → π* transitions of different functionalities (C = O, C-N) (Chernyak et al., 2020), which came from the decomposition product of protein. The protein removed to the solvent increased with time as shown in the 3.2.4 section. The intensity of peak at 408–420 nm reflected the content of conjugated polymers on the surface of CDs, and CDs obtained from DMF-assisted hydrothermal at 200℃ for 7 h showed a wide and high peak.

The fluorescence spectrum of CDs only showed an emission peak at 670 nm before the pretreatment 3 h. After 5 h, the second peak gradually appeared at 620 nm, and gradually increased with time, indicating that the new group was formed and caused the double emission peak behavior with the prolonging of pretreated time. As the time increases from 1 h to 9 h, the main emission peak is blue-shifted from 675 nm to 648 nm. It may be caused by the continuous decrease in the size of the CDs (Liu et al., 2019). The CDs obtained at different pretreatment time all have the strongest excitation peak at 400 nm and excitation-dependent fluorescence characteristics after 3 h. Excitation-dependent behavior is usually attributed to size distribution and surface chemistry, or different emission traps (rescue effect) (Krysmann et al., 2012). After 7 h pretreatment, when the temperature is lower than 200℃, the fluorescence emission spectrum is dominated by the red light emission peak above 600 nm. The fluorescence emission peak appears at about 460 nm at 220℃, which causes yellow fluorescence of the solution under 365 nm ultraviolet light irradiation. It can be observed that the emission energy at 430 nm is much higher than it at above 620 nm under 360 nm excitation wavelength. Subsequently, as the excitation wavelength increases to 420 nm, the intensity of blue fluorescence gradually decreased, while red fluorescence reached a maximum at 400 nm excitation and then it decreased. Under 360 nm excitation, when the temperature is 240℃, the emission spectrum is in the blue-violet wavelength range. It may be related to the lack of surface functional groups and the highly carbonized structure at high temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructure and surface properties analysis of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and size of the CDs were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As the DMF pretreated time and temperature increased, the size of the resulting CDs decreased. For example, CDs pretreated in DMF at 200℃ for 5 h with an an average diameter of 74.72 nm, and 73.33 nm at 200℃ for 9 h. This result is due to the shedding of functional groups on the surface and further carbonization of CDs as time and temperature increased. Moreover, the samples of HTL-200℃-5h and DMF-240℃-7h revealed clear lattice fringes of CDs with a spacing of 0.24 nm and 0.32 nm. The lattice spacing of 0.24 nm is related to the in-plane (100) lattice spacing of graphitic structure, and the interlayer distance of graphitic carbons is approximately 0.34 nm.

To obtain further insights into the synthesized CDs, XPS analysis was carried out at 284.8 eV for C 1 s, 399.8 eV for N 1 s and 531.4 eV for O 1 s. The atomic percentages of C, O, and N are 76.25%, 17.44%, and 6.31%, respectively. When the CDs from high protein contained biomass(milk, hair, etc), they always have nitrogen groups (Wang and Zhou, 2014; Sun et al., 2013). The high-resolution C1s spectrum showed three C-types including C–C/C = C (284.8 eV), C-O/C-N (286.23 eV), and C = O (287.73 eV) (Elsayed et al., 2021; Tang et al., 2021). The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum showed three peaks located at 398.73 eV, 399.78 eV and 400.73 eV, corresponding to the nitrogen atoms in C = N-C (pyridine N), C-N–H (amino group) and C-N-C (pyrrolic N), respectively (Zhou et al., 2020). Compared to the nitrogen doping CDs synthesized with urea (Tang et al., 2021), the nitrogen in CDs from Chlorella was predominantly amino N rather than N-C(3). The O1s spectrum exhibited two binding energies at 532.4 eV and 531.08 eV, which can be assigned to C-O and C = O, respectively. The results revealed that the CDs surface was equipped with –OH and –NH2 as function groups. These surface functional groups also had a contribution to the CDs size, making the CDs bigger than graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbon nanodots (CNDs), which were similar to polymer dots (Pdots).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: Sugar, nitrogenous compounds and carbon dots (CDs) were obtained via the hydrothermal pretreatment of Chlorella in DMF solvent. The results showed the sugars and monomeric sugars (levoglucose) in bio-oil, reaching 41.59% and 29.17%, respectively. The nitrogen heterocycles in the bio-oil were mainly from the ring formation reaction of amino acids. And the gradual increase of nitrile after 5 h hydrothermal pretreatment is attributed to the dehydration of amides in carbonpolymer during pyrolysis and the solvent is involved in the reaction during carbonpolymer. The red fluorescence of CDs is due to the abundance of conjugated polymers on the surface. “E-supplementary data for this work can be found in e-version of this paper online”.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
ce:section.ce:para: Linghao Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Fan Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology. Ren Tu: Formal analysis. Haipeng Yu: Formal analysis, Methodology. Hong Wang: Formal analysis, Methodology. Yan Sun: Formal analysis, Investigation. Enchen Jiang: Resources, Supervision. Xiwei Xu: Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (Grant No.52076083); Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province, China (Grant No.2021A1515010886); Youth science and technology innovation talent of Guangdong TeZhi plan China (Grant No. 2019TQ05N068); and Rural Revitalization Strategy Special Fund (“Special Project + Task List”) project in Yunfu City, Guangdong Province, China (Grant No. 2020020203).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2021.126143.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
